ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'rice pudding' class (FOODON_03301116); no specific Phirni entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class. Ground-rice preparation distinguishes Phirni from whole-grain kheer.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers rice pudding generically. No Phirni-specific code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Mapped to closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Phirni or North Indian rice pudding as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Phirni is approximately 150 g, typically presented in a small shallow earthen clay bowl (shikora), which is the traditional vessel used to set and serve this North Indian rice pudding.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides compositional data for milk-based rice desserts including kheer and similar preparations. Phirni values are adapted from the closest NIN entry for rice-milk pudding (kheer/rice pudding) with adjustment for higher sugar content and ground-rice preparation. Saturated fat estimated from full-cream milk fat composition per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole milk, fluid). Cholesterol estimated from full-cream milk contribution per USDA FoodData Central. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown for this traditional preparation is not reported in NIN or USDA for this specific dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice pudding and milk-rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Phirni found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is estimated by analogy to rice pudding (whole milk, sugar) entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which report GI values of 58–66 for milk-based rice puddings. The combination of full-cream milk fat and protein moderates glucose absorption relative to plain cooked rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 33.75) / 100 = 20.9 (rounded). Carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 22.5 × 1.5 = 33.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (40 mg/100 g); moderate potassium (145 mg/100 g) and calcium (115 mg/100 g) from full-cream milk
hypertension.guidance: Phirni is naturally low in sodium and provides potassium and calcium from milk, making it acceptable in moderate portions for individuals managing hypertension, though saturated fat intake from full-cream milk should be monitored and total caloric load managed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar (18 g/100 g) and refined ground rice carbohydrates (22.5 g/100 g) with a medium GI of 62 and glycemic load of ~20.9 per standard 150 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Phirni carries a high concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its substantial added sugar and refined carbohydrate content; strict portion control is essential, and sugar-free or reduced-sugar preparations using low-GI sweeteners should be considered to limit postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from full-cream milk (~4.1 g/100 g) and high sugar content (18 g/100 g) contributing to atherogenic risk if consumed frequently
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Phirni to occasional small servings, as the saturated fat from full-cream milk and high sugar content can adversely affect lipid profiles and contribute to caloric surplus; substituting low-fat milk and reducing added sugar would lower cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar intake may promote systemic inflammation; dairy may be pro-inflammatory in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimize refined sugar intake and monitor dairy tolerance, as both may exacerbate inflammatory responses; Phirni should be consumed sparingly and only if dairy is well tolerated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Phirni (also spelled Firni or Ferni) is a classic North Indian milk-based rice dessert, deeply rooted in Mughlai and Kashmiri culinary traditions. It is prepared by slow-cooking coarsely ground raw Basmati rice — soaked and blended to a coarse paste — in full-cream whole milk over low heat, stirring continuously until the mixture thickens to a smooth, creamy, and uniform pudding consistency. Unlike Kheer, which uses whole rice grains and results in a more textured pudding, Phirni's use of ground rice produces a distinctly smoother, denser, and more velvety texture. The pudding is sweetened with sugar and flavored with saffron strands, freshly ground green cardamom, and rose water or screwpine (kewra) water, imparting a fragrant floral aroma. It is traditionally poured into shallow unglazed earthen clay bowls called 'shikoras', which absorb excess moisture and impart a subtle earthy note, then chilled until set. Garnished with slivered almonds and pistachios, Phirni is a staple dessert during Ramadan Iftar, Eid-ul-Fitr, and North Indian weddings and banquets. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 168 kcal, 4.6 g protein, 6.8 g fat (predominantly saturated from full-cream milk), and 22.5 g carbohydrates with 18 g sugar. It is a good source of calcium (115 mg) and potassium (145 mg) from milk. Its medium glycemic index (~62) and high glycemic load per serving make it unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing cardiovascular risk. It is gluten-free and vegetarian, and is halal-compliant in its traditional formulation.
common_user_queries: How is Phirni different from Kheer?
What are the ingredients in Phirni?
What is the glycemic index of Phirni?
Can I eat Phirni if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for milk-based rice desserts (closest approved source match for Indian traditional preparations); no exact Phirni entry exists in any approved primary source. Saturated fat and cholesterol are estimated from full-cream milk composition per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice pudding entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' for milk-rice pudding (kheer-type) as the closest approved source; no exact Phirni entry exists in ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Substitution documented in nutrient_source. 2. Saturated fat (4.1 g/100 g) estimated from full-cream milk fat composition per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole milk, fluid, USDA #01077); approximately 60% of total fat in full-cream milk is saturated. 3. Cholesterol (22 mg/100 g) estimated from full-cream milk contribution per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this traditional ground-rice milk pudding preparation are not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish as of 2024. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on rice pudding entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for Phirni exists in approved sources. 6. Portions array in input contained four identical duplicate entries (150 g); deduplicated to one canonical serving of 150 g. 7. food_group corrected from 'Snacks' to 'Desserts' to accurately reflect the nature of Phirni as a milk-based rice pudding dessert. 8. Input alias language code 'ka' appears to be an error (ka = Kannada, not Kashmiri); the script پھِرنی is Nastaliq/Urdu-Kashmiri script; retained with note that Kashmiri ISO 639-3 code is 'ks'. 9. No core macro fields are zero; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
